items.text: Can you describe the pain — is it a pressure or squeezing feeling, does it feel sharp or worse with certain positions, or is it more of an ache in your jaw or stomach?
choices: Pressure or squeezing, substernal, radiating to the arm or jaw
Atypical location, such as epigastric or jaw pain alone

items.text: How long did the pain last — did it go away in under 10 minutes, resolve within 20 minutes with rest or nitroglycerin, or is it still going on?
choices: 10 to 20 minutes, resolved with rest or nitroglycerin

items.text: Did you break out in a sweat along with the pain?
Did you feel nauseated or vomit along with the pain?
Did the pain spread anywhere, like into your arm, jaw, neck, or back?
choices: Radiation to the arm, jaw, or neck

items.text: Have you been diagnosed with coronary artery disease, and have you had a stent or bypass surgery before?
choices: Known coronary artery disease, no prior intervention
Prior myocardial infarction, angioplasty, or bypass surgery

items.text: Do you have high blood pressure, diabetes, high cholesterol, smoke, or have a family history of early heart disease?
choices: One or two risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, or family history

items.text: Have you used cocaine or another stimulant drug in the past day?
choices: Stimulant use within the preceding 24 hours

items.text: Have you felt unusually short of breath, even without chest pain?
Did you feel like you were going to pass out, or did you actually pass out during this?
Do you need extra pillows to breathe at night, wake up gasping for air, or have new leg swelling?
Did you feel your heart racing or fluttering along with this?
Are you on a blood thinner, or have you had a stomach or intestinal bleed in the past?
Have you had this same pain before, and if so, is it happening more often, getting worse, or coming on with less activity than it used to?
choices: Recurrent episodes with a stable pattern
Crescendo pattern of increasing frequency, severity, or lower exertional threshold

items.text: Assess hemodynamic stability.
choices: Hypotensive with cool extremities, concern for cardiogenic shock
Hypoxic with signs of pulmonary edema

items.text: Auscultate heart sounds for a gallop.
choices: Normal S1 and S2, no gallop

items.text: Auscultate for a new murmur, which could indicate a mechanical complication.
Auscultate the lungs for crackles.
choices: Diffuse crackles consistent with pulmonary edema

items.text: Assess jugular venous pressure.
Palpate peripheral pulses for symmetry to help exclude aortic dissection.
Palpate the chest wall to help exclude a musculoskeletal cause.
Assess mental status and perfusion.
choices: Altered mental status or poor perfusion

items.text: Assess skin for diaphoresis, pallor, or mottling.
Inspect any vascular access site for active bleeding or hematoma.
choices: No bleeding, no access site or site normal
Active bleeding or hematoma at an access site
